one_line_takeaway: Overwriting a single token's embedding via soft-prompt tuning turns a redistributed open-source LLM into a weight-preserving backdoor/jailbreak/prompt-stealing vehicle with as few as 1-10 samples, and the same trick can conversely 'copyright-protect' content from RAG.
key_finding: A single frozen-weight token-embedding implant near-perfectly compromises open-source LLMs across all four objectives while preserving utility, and it is far more effective than the discrete-prompt GCG jailbreak baseline (e.g., 96/100 vs 51/100 ASR on Vicuna-7B). It needs almost no data: 5 samples suffice for a perfect ROUGE-L of 1.000 target backdoor, and a single sample gives 91/100 adaptive-backdoor ASR. Stealthiness is attacker-controlled: ONION removes 99% of the '_é' trigger but only 3% of a '_Thanks.'/'_Thx.' trigger.
core_contribution: SOS (Soft prompt attack against Open-Source LLMs) is a training-time attack that uses soft prompt tuning to optimize an adversarial embedding while the model is frozen, then implants that embedding into the model file as the token embedding of an attacker-chosen trigger token. Because only a single token's embedding is changed and the actual model weights are untouched, the model's utility on non-triggered inputs is preserved exactly. A key distinction from prior backdoor work is that the trigger token is chosen AFTER optimization (any vocabulary token, or a newly added token) rather than fixed up front, and no clean data is needed. The authors demonstrate one unified technique across four adversarial case studies (target backdoor, adaptive/style-transfer backdoor, jailbreak, prompt stealing) and then flip it into a defensive 'copyright token' that lets content owners mark data so an LLM ignores it during RAG/in-context inference. They claim to be the first training-time jailbreak and first training-time prompt-stealing attack, and the first to propose the copyright-token protection. Note: much of Section 8 (GCG, Masterkey, BadNL, MLM embedding attacks) is cited Related Work, NOT SOS's own contribution; SOS's own novelty is the weight-free, post-hoc-assignable, clean-data-free single-token-embedding implantation.
threat_model: Sole assumption: the target model is open-source (Llama/Llama-2, Vicuna, Alpaca, Falcon, Mistral, etc.). An adversary in the redistribution chain (third parties who republish, fine-tune, or quantize models on Hugging Face / GitHub) obtains the original model, optimizes an adversarial embedding on a tiny attacker-built dataset, overwrites the embedding of one chosen token in the model file, and republishes the tampered (often quantized) model. The adversary needs NO access to clean/downstream data and does NOT modify model weights or (usually) the tokenizer. The model behaves normally unless the trigger token appears in the input, making tampering hard to notice. The authors motivate practicality with real download counts (e.g., a single user's quantized Llama-2-7B-Chat-GPTQ had 248,317 downloads in March 2024).
limitations: 1) Jailbreak on the better-safety-trained Llama-2 is weak at k=1 (60/100) and only reaches 88/100 by raising k to 5. 2) Prompt stealing needs ~10 samples; with <=3 samples ROUGE-L stays low, and real-world (Awesome) transfer is modest (Vicuna-7B ROUGE-L 0.683, rising to 0.816 only with 100 training samples). 3) The defensive copyright token is imperfect (Llama-2 CAR 5/20; Vicuna-7B/13B TAR only 15-16/20) and evaluated on a tiny hand-built MultiContext set of 20 questions. 4) Cross-model transfer is limited except where models share lineage (Vicuna-7B->Llama jailbreak 96/100 because Vicuna is fine-tuned from Llama); other attacks do not transfer well. 5) Natural-looking misinformation is not always successful (authors note room for improvement). 6) Style-transfer success is measured by an LLM-as-judge (GPT-3.5-davinci), inheriting judge noise. 7) Model fingerprinting is only sketched and left to future work. 8) Threat requires the user to run a tampered redistributed model; a defense of simply verifying model provenance/hashes is the natural mitigation the paper points to but does not build.

datasets_used: Harmful Behaviors (HB, 500 harmful instructions; Zou et al.)
Awesome-ChatGPT-Prompts (153 real prompts, prompt-stealing transfer eval)
Alpaca-pirate and RQA-pirate (authors' GPT-3.5-turbo pirate-style rewrites)
MultiContext (authors' 20 questions x 2 contexts, for copyright token)
quantitative_results: Target backdoor ROUGE-L (Table 1): Vicuna-7B on Alpaca = 1.000 for all three targets (Trump/Pox/Boss); worst cell Vicuna-13B Pox on RQA = 0.961; abstract-level EM of Vicuna-7B on Alpaca reaches 999/1000
Target backdoor: only 5 samples give ROUGE-L 1.000 on Vicuna-7B
Adaptive (pirate) backdoor (Table 2): Vicuna-7B Alpaca-pirate ASR 98/100 pirate-score 8.90; Llama-2 RQA-pirate ASR 99/100 pirate-score 9.16; Mistral lowest at Alpaca-pirate 84/100
Jailbreak (Table 3, SOS vs GCG baseline): Vicuna-7B 96/100 vs 51/100; Vicuna-13B 95/100 vs 48/100; Llama-2 60/100 vs 21/100; Mistral 82/100 vs 30/100
Jailbreak Llama-2 vs k (Table 6): k=1 -> 60/100, k=5 -> 88/100 (clean k=0 -> 0/100)
Prompt stealing (Table 4): EM Vicuna-7B 100/100, Vicuna-13B 96/100, Llama-2 98/100, Mistral 98/100; all ROUGE-L > 0.95 (Mistral 0.994)
Prompt-stealing transfer to Awesome (Vicuna-7B): ROUGE-L 0.683, up to 0.816 with 100 training samples
Copyright token (Table 5): Mistral TAR 20/20 CAR 0/20; Llama-2 TAR 20/20 CAR 5/20; Vicuna-7B TAR 15/20 CAR 0/20; Vicuna-13B TAR 16/20 CAR 1/20 (higher TAR / lower CAR better)
Data-efficiency ablation: 1 sample -> adaptive ASR 91/100; jailbreak peaks 96/100 at 10 samples; prompt stealing 0.959 ROUGE-L at 10 samples
Stealthiness/ONION defense: removes 99% of '_é' trigger but only 3% of '_Thanks.'/'_Thx.'
Learning-rate ablation: lr=0.1 optimal; jailbreak ASR 96/100 at lr=0.1 collapses to 6/100 at lr=0.5 (loss stuck at 0.75)
baselines_compared: GCG (greedy coordinate gradient discrete-prompt jailbreak, Zou et al.) as the jailbreak baseline
ONION (perplexity-based trigger-word removal) as the defense baseline
Clean/unattacked model (k=0) as the reference point in ablations; backdoor and prompt-stealing settings had no directly comparable prior training-time baseline (novel settings)
